topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on the screen, stark white against the muted grey of my laptop. *For Sale: 14 Willow Creek Lane.* My breath hitched, a sudden, shallow catch in my chest. I hadn't checked that listing in years, not since I moved across the country, yet the familiar address hit me like a physical blow. My fingers, usually steady, began to tremble as I reread the details: the overgrown garden, the peeling paint on the porch railing, the exact layout of the rooms I used to hide in. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, pulling my shoulders up toward my ears. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small living room, the floorboards protesting beneath my hurried steps. I needed to call someone, anyone, but the phone felt heavy, glued to my palm. Every creak of the house settling in the night seemed amplified, a sound that whispered secrets I wasn't ready to hear.

[story 2]

I stared at the photograph—a faded image of the front door, slightly warped by time. My jaw locked so tight I could feel the strain in my molars. It was impossible. That house had been a closed chapter, neatly filed away under 'memories.' Now, it was resurfacing, an open wound in the landscape of my present. I walked over to the window, pulling the curtain back just enough to peer down the street. The familiar oak tree stood exactly where it always had, but everything else felt subtly shifted, charged with an unwelcome energy. My hands started to sweat, slicking the skin of my palms. I kept wiping them on my jeans, a nervous habit I hadn't indulged in since I was a teenager. Why now? The question echoed in the silence of my apartment, sharp and insistent.

[story 3]

The screen glowed, illuminating the pale sheen of anxiety rising on my forehead. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. Willow Creek Lane. It was a phantom address, a place steeped in the residue of youth, and now it was being offered up for transaction. I found myself compulsively refreshing the page, a frantic little loop of clicking and reading. My heart began a quick, uneven rhythm against my ribs. I glanced around my living space, suddenly hyper-aware of every shadow stretching across the wall, every distant siren sounding too loud. I needed distance, needed to sever the connection before whatever this was could start to unravel everything I’d built. I stood up, moving toward the door, my movements jerky and too fast.
